From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Indie electronic genre comprising elements of indie folk and electronica

"Folktronic" redirects here. For the Momus album, see [Folktronic (album)](/wiki/Folktronic_(album) "Folktronic (album)").

| Folktronica | |
| --- | --- |
| Stylistic origins | * [Electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica") * [folk](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music") * [downtempo](/wiki/Downtempo "Downtempo") * [glitch](/wiki/Glitch_(music) "Glitch (music)") * [IDM](/wiki/Intelligent_dance_music "Intelligent dance music") * [indie music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music") * [post-rock](/wiki/Post-rock "Post-rock") |
| Cultural origins | 1990s–2000s[[1]](#cite_note-nf-1) |
| Typical instruments | * [Electronic](/wiki/Electronic_musical_instrument "Electronic musical instrument") * [acoustic](/wiki/Acoustic_instrument "Acoustic instrument") |
| Other topics | |
| * [Ethnic electronica](/wiki/Ethnic_electronica "Ethnic electronica") * [freak folk](/wiki/Freak_folk "Freak folk") | |

**Folktronica**[[1]](#cite_note-nf-1) is a genre of [indie electronic](/wiki/Electronic_music#Indie_electronic "Electronic music")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] music comprising various elements of [folk music](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music") and [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica"), often featuring uses of [acoustic instruments](/wiki/Acoustic_instruments "Acoustic instruments")—especially stringed instruments—deploying [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music"), [electronic](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") or [dance](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music") rhythms, and incorporating delicate, textural [sound design](/wiki/Sound_design "Sound design"), although it varies based on influences and choice of sounds.[[1]](#cite_note-nf-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Empire-2) *The Ashgate Research Companion to Popular Musicology* describes folktronica as "a catch-all [term] for all manner of artists who have combined mechanical dance beats with elements of acoustic rock or folk".[[3]](#cite_note-Scott/Ashgate-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folktronica&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The 1991 album *[Every Man and Woman is a Star](/wiki/Every_Man_and_Woman_is_a_Star "Every Man and Woman is a Star")* by [Ultramarine](/wiki/Ultramarine_(band) "Ultramarine (band)") was described in a 2014 [PopMatters](/wiki/PopMatters "PopMatters") article as a progenitor of the genre;[[4]](#cite_note-4) it featured a [pastoral](/wiki/Pastoral "Pastoral") sound and incorporated traditional instruments such as violin and harmonica with [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno") and [house](/wiki/House_music "House music") elements. The term *folktronica* was coined in the late 1990s, presumably first by the British press in reference to the musician [Kieran Hebden](/wiki/Kieran_Hebden "Kieran Hebden") ([Four Tet](/wiki/Four_Tet "Four Tet")).[[5]](#cite_note-5) In the early 2000s, indie electronic acts such as [Múm](/wiki/M%C3%BAm "Múm"), Four Tet, [Isan](/wiki/Isan_(band) "Isan (band)"), [Bibio](/wiki/Bibio "Bibio") and [the Books](/wiki/The_Books "The Books") began releasing formative music in the genre.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6)

According to *The Sunday Times Culture's Encyclopedia of Modern Music*, essential albums of the genre are Four Tet's [*Pause*](/wiki/Pause_(Four_Tet_album) "Pause (Four Tet album)") (2001) and [*Rounds*](/wiki/Rounds_(album) "Rounds (album)") (2003), [Tunng](/wiki/Tunng "Tunng")'s *[Mother's Daughter and Other Songs](/wiki/Mother%27s_Daughter_and_Other_Songs "Mother's Daughter and Other Songs")* (2005), and [Caribou](/wiki/Caribou_(musician) "Caribou (musician)")'s *[The Milk of Human Kindness](/wiki/The_Milk_of_Human_Kindness "The Milk of Human Kindness")* (2005),[[7]](#cite_note-TimesOnline.co.UK:EoMM-7) some of which had a cross-genre influence on artists such as [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen"), [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead") and [J Dilla](/wiki/J_Dilla "J Dilla").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In addition to its roots in underground indie and electronic music, some commentators have noted that elements of folktronica entered the mainstream through pop artists who experimented with blending acoustic textures and electronic production. [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") incorporated aspects of folktronica into her early-2000s work, particularly on her 2003 album *[American Life](/wiki/American_Life "American Life")*, after starting to explore a similar genre-crossing by blending country and dance-pop on *[Music](/wiki/Music_(Madonna_album) "Music (Madonna album)")* (2000). She has been credited with helping to introduce the hybrid sound to a broader audience beyond the indie scene.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
